addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

1	//! tprobe — a `no_std`-first hardware tick-counter measurement
2	//! probe.
3	//!
4	//! Reads a hardware tick counter and records deltas through a
5	//! pluggable [`Recorder`] (collection phase); analysis and
6	//! presentation happen off the measured path — see the "Phases"
7	//! section of `notes/design.md`:
8	//!
9	//! - [`ticks`] — per-arch tick counter (`rdtsc`,
10	//!   `CNTVCT_EL0`); reads are `no_std`, calibration and
11	//!   validation are `std`-gated
12	//! - [`Recorder`] / [`ArrayRecorder`] — where deltas go;
13	//!   no-alloc, embeddable on-target
14	//! - [`TProbe`] — the collection front end
15	//!   (`start()` … `record()`)
16	//!
17	//! Founding rationale and design live in `notes/design.md`. The
18	//! core is built out over the `0.1.0` ladder; `examples/tp_pc`
19	//! is the parity port of iiac-perf's `tp-pc` bench.
20	#![cfg_attr(not(feature = "std"), no_std)]
21

23	pub mod recorder;

content.content:
97	## The design: no_std core + std feature layer
98	
99	This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
100	relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
101	be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:
102	
103	- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
104	  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
105	  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
106	  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
107	  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
108	  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
109	- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
110	  needs no allocator.
111	- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
112	  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
113	  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
114	  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
115	- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
116	  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
117	  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
118	  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
119	  build gets just the core.
120	- **Spans** — dissolved, not folded in. The copies split
121	  TProbe/TProbe2 (`tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf, `tprobe_span.rs`
122	  in zc-ring) because one histogrammed inline and one buffered
123	  records; the Recorder split removes that reason. The span
124	  API's `site_id` goes with it: TProbe2 kept one buffer and one
125	  histogram for many sites, so its `site_id` was dead code
126	  awaiting "per-site grouping" — which a probe per site (the
127	  `&'static str` name is the site) gives for free. What might
128	  return later, on the one probe type:
129	  - a caller-held start token (`start_span() -> Span`,
130	    `record_span(Span)`) for overlapping scopes on a single
131	    probe — rare once probes are per-site; add when a consumer
132	    needs it;
133	  - time-ordered cross-site tracing — a future recorder
134	    sample-type question (`(id, start, end)` trace records, the
135	    flight-recorder use the Phases section notes), not a probe
136	    question.
137	
138	## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation
139	
140	Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
141	measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
142	**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
143	and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
144	use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
145	data is never presented on the device. The existing copies fold
146	analysis into collection (histogram update on the hot path,
147	rendering in-process); this design separates three phases and
148	keeps everything but raw collection off the measured path:
149	
150	- **Collection** — the hot path does a tick read, a delta, and
151	  one recorder operation; nothing else. The recorder is a
152	  pluggable trait with three `no_std` impls:
153	  - `ArrayRecorder` — preallocated delta array, one sequential
154	    store per event. For benchmarks: bounded iteration counts
155	    and a natural "after" mean no concurrent thread is needed;
156	    analysis runs after the loop with zero overlap with any
157	    measurement.
158	  - `RingRecorder` — raw deltas into an SPSC ring drained by a
159	    collector thread. For instrumented apps with spare cores:
160	    at 10M events/s the raw stream is ~80 MB/s, trivial against
161	    within-machine memory bandwidth. Shipping buffers between
162	    threads is zc-ring's domain — a natural composition, though
163	    transport stays out of this crate.
164	  - `HistogramRecorder` — in-place log-linear bucket increment.
165	    For embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth
166	    is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots
167	    instead.
168	- **Analysis** — histogram build and percentile extraction.
169	  Always off the measured path: after the loop (array), on the
170	  collector thread (ring), or off-device (histogram snapshots).
171	- **Presentation** — band-table rendering, formatting, tick→ns
172	  display. `std`-only, operates on snapshots, never on the
173	  measuring device.
174	
175	The organizing insight: **histogramming is compression, not
176	analysis**. A log-linear histogram compresses the delta
177	distribution, lossy only in bucket resolution, and it is the
178	interchange format between phases wherever compression happens.
179	The recorder choice just moves the compression point — after the
180	run, on a collector core, or on the device — so analysis and
181	presentation downstream are identical in all three modes.
182	
183	Consequences for the core:
184	
185	- **Snapshot/drain is a first-class operation** — wait-free for
186	  the recorder (double-buffered counts or an epoch swap), so a
187	  collector can harvest without blocking the hot path.
188	- **A small versioned `no_std` wire format** for histogram
189	  snapshots (bucket-layout parameters + counts) is the boundary
190	  between this crate and the world.
191	- **Transport is out of scope** — the probe produces snapshots
192	  and raw rings; which thread, socket, or UART moves them is
193	  the application's business.
194	- Raw recorders retain **time ordering** that histograms
195	  discard (correlating spikes with events, periodic patterns) —
196	  a flight-recorder use distinct from distribution measurement,
197	  and a reason the raw modes are first-class rather than merely
198	  an optimization.
199	
200	### Outer loop as cross-check, not measurement
201	
202	For the benchmark harness (iiac-perf), summing the recorded
203	inner deltas gives the duration directly, which demotes the
204	outer-loop timing from "the measurement" to a cross-check worth
205	keeping:
206	
207	- `sum(inner deltas)` — time actually spent in the measured op;
208	  the right numerator for op throughput.
209	- `outer − sum(inner)` — the per-iteration apparatus cost (tick
210	  reads, the record store, loop overhead), *measured* for that
211	  exact run. Today the copies estimate this with a separate
212	  calibration pass (actor-x1's `overhead.rs`); this makes it a
213	  free per-run self-check that calibration matches reality.
214	
215	### First experiment: recorder cost
216	
217	We think the raw array/ring store is cheaper per event than the
218	histogram increment (a store vs. leading-zeros + shift + counter
219	bump), but the histogram's table stays L1-resident while a raw
220	array streams through cache — for long runs the eviction
221	pressure could invert the ranking. Comparing recorder impls is
222	the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against
223	itself, folded into probe-overhead calibration.
224	
225	## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer
226

## The design: no_std core + std feature layer

This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:

- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
  needs no allocator.
- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
  build gets just the core.
- **Spans** — dissolved, not folded in. The copies split
  TProbe/TProbe2 (`tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf, `tprobe_span.rs`
  in zc-ring) because one histogrammed inline and one buffered
  records; the Recorder split removes that reason. The span
  API's `site_id` goes with it: TProbe2 kept one buffer and one
  histogram for many sites, so its `site_id` was dead code
  awaiting "per-site grouping" — which a probe per site (the
  `&'static str` name is the site) gives for free. What might
  return later, on the one probe type:
  - a caller-held start token (`start_span() -> Span`,
    `record_span(Span)`) for overlapping scopes on a single
    probe — rare once probes are per-site; add when a consumer
    needs it;
  - time-ordered cross-site tracing — a future recorder
    sample-type question (`(id, start, end)` trace records, the
    flight-recorder use the Phases section notes), not a probe
    question.

## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation

Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
data is never presented on the device. The existing copies fold
analysis into collection (histogram update on the hot path,
rendering in-process); this design separates three phases and
keeps everything but raw collection off the measured path:

- **Collection** — the hot path does a tick read, a delta, and
  one recorder operation; nothing else. The recorder is a
  pluggable trait with three `no_std` impls:
  - `ArrayRecorder` — preallocated delta array, one sequential
    store per event. For benchmarks: bounded iteration counts
    and a natural "after" mean no concurrent thread is needed;
    analysis runs after the loop with zero overlap with any
    measurement.
  - `RingRecorder` — raw deltas into an SPSC ring drained by a
    collector thread. For instrumented apps with spare cores:
    at 10M events/s the raw stream is ~80 MB/s, trivial against
    within-machine memory bandwidth. Shipping buffers between
    threads is zc-ring's domain — a natural composition, though
    transport stays out of this crate.
  - `HistogramRecorder` — in-place log-linear bucket increment.
    For embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth
    is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots
    instead.
- **Analysis** — histogram build and percentile extraction.
  Always off the measured path: after the loop (array), on the
  collector thread (ring), or off-device (histogram snapshots).
- **Presentation** — band-table rendering, formatting, tick→ns
  display. `std`-only, operates on snapshots, never on the
  measuring device.

The organizing insight: **histogramming is compression, not
analysis**. A log-linear histogram compresses the delta
distribution, lossy only in bucket resolution, and it is the
interchange format between phases wherever compression happens.
The recorder choice just moves the compression point — after the
run, on a collector core, or on the device — so analysis and
presentation downstream are identical in all three modes.

Consequences for the core:

- **Snapshot/drain is a first-class operation** — wait-free for
  the recorder (double-buffered counts or an epoch swap), so a
  collector can harvest without blocking the hot path.
- **A small versioned `no_std` wire format** for histogram
  snapshots (bucket-layout parameters + counts) is the boundary
  between this crate and the world.
- **Transport is out of scope** — the probe produces snapshots
  and raw rings; which thread, socket, or UART moves them is
  the application's business.
- Raw recorders retain **time ordering** that histograms
  discard (correlating spikes with events, periodic patterns) —
  a flight-recorder use distinct from distribution measurement,
  and a reason the raw modes are first-class rather than merely
  an optimization.

### Outer loop as cross-check, not measurement

For the benchmark harness (iiac-perf), summing the recorded
inner deltas gives the duration directly, which demotes the
outer-loop timing from "the measurement" to a cross-check worth
keeping:

- `sum(inner deltas)` — time actually spent in the measured op;
  the right numerator for op throughput.
- `outer − sum(inner)` — the per-iteration apparatus cost (tick
  reads, the record store, loop overhead), *measured* for that
  exact run. Today the copies estimate this with a separate
  calibration pass (actor-x1's `overhead.rs`); this makes it a
  free per-run self-check that calibration matches reality.

### First experiment: recorder cost

We think the raw array/ring store is cheaper per event than the
histogram increment (a store vs. leading-zeros + shift + counter
bump), but the histogram's table stays L1-resident while a raw
array streams through cache — for long runs the eviction
pressure could invert the ranking. Comparing recorder impls is
the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against
itself, folded into probe-overhead calibration.

content.text:
Your sketch lines up almost point-for-point with what `notes/design.md` already commits to — which is a good sign the model is stable. Let me map it, then flag the three things in your framing that are genuinely new design questions.

**Where it matches the landed design:**

- "Placed in various locations, named, recorded as efficiently as possible" — that's the collection phase as built: `TProbe` with a `&'static str` name, hot path = tick read + delta + one `Recorder::record` call, nothing else.
- "Background task processes into a histogram, concurrently in a live app" — that's the planned `RingRecorder`: raw deltas into an SPSC ring, a collector thread drains and histograms off the measured path.
- "Or periodically in a benchmark between loops" — that's `ArrayRecorder`, already landed: bounded run, analyze after the loop, no concurrent thread needed.
- The design's organizing insight covers why both modes downstream look identical: histogramming is compression, and the recorder choice just moves the compression point (after the run, on a collector core, or on-device).

**Where your framing raises questions design.md hasn't answered:**

1. **"Sent the current set of tprobes" implies discovery.** Today nothing knows the set of probes — each is a local value owned by its call site, and the design's stance is "transport is out of scope." For a background task to harvest *the set*, either (a) the app wires it explicitly — creating a probe with a ring recorder also yields a consumer handle you hand to the collector, name traveling with the handle — or (b) there's a global registry, which in a no-alloc `no_std` core means a fixed-capacity static table and some `Sync` care. My lean: explicit wiring in the core (it's just the SPSC consumer end), with a convenience registry as a possible `std`-layer addition later. That keeps the core honest and matches the "probe produces, app moves" boundary.

2. **Push vs drain.** "Periodically sent the current set of events" sounds like batch handoff — the probe gives up a full buffer and gets an empty one. The design instead has the collector *pull* from a ring continuously, which needs no coordination on the hot path. A swap scheme is viable too, but someone has to initiate the swap, and any hot-path "should I swap?" check is overhead the ring design avoids. I'd treat the ring as the mechanism and "periodic" as merely how often the collector wakes up to drain — same observable behavior, cheaper hot path.

3. **"Singular events" needs pinning down.** Spans are the current API (`start()`…`record()` → a delta). But what does a singular event record? If it's "an occurrence at time T," that's a timestamped trace record — design.md explicitly defers that as a future recorder sample-type question (the flight-recorder use). If it's "time since the last occurrence," that's inter-arrival measurement and falls out of the existing API almost for free (`record()` then `start()`, or a `lap()`-style combined read). Worth deciding which you mean before it shapes the recorder wire format.

One caution on the live-app path: the ring's producer side must stay wait-free and single-writer per probe — `TProbe` is deliberately `!Sync`, and I'd keep it that way; a probe per thread per site, not a shared probe, is what keeps the record path to a plain store.

So: nothing in your description contradicts the plan; the concrete next design work it surfaces is the `RingRecorder` + collector handoff (with explicit consumer-handle wiring) and a decision on what a singular event's sample is. Want me to write these up as Todo entries, or start a design subsection in chores for the ring/collector shape?

and gets a set of buffers to fill. It's then used at a particular location
and periodically "notifies" the registry that the buffer a buffer ready to
be collected. This could be sending a buffer or just setting a bit in the

content.text:
This works, and I think it's stronger than it might first appear because your two cases collapse into one mechanism. Here's how I'd frame it:

**The core is a buffer-cycling protocol, not two schemes.** "Registry hands the probe a set of buffers" plus "notify when one is ready" is a free-list/ready-list pair: the probe holds one active buffer, fills it with plain stores, and on full pushes it to *ready* and pulls a fresh one from *free*; the collector drains *ready*, histograms, and returns buffers to *free*. Your two notify variants are the same ownership handoff — "sending a buffer" is the queue view, "setting a bit" is the in-place view where the buffer never moves and an atomic per-slot state (FILLING → READY → FREE) signals the transfer. In a `no_std` core they're both just atomics; whether the collector *polls* the bits or gets *woken* (eventfd, condvar) is a `std`-layer concern on top of the same protocol.

**Both use classes are the same protocol driven differently:**

- **Benchmark** — the "collector" is inline: at a well-defined point the harness calls `registry.collect()` on its own thread. No concurrency, and with one big buffer per probe it degenerates to exactly today's `ArrayRecorder` behavior — nice continuity, the landed code becomes the 1-buffer special case.
- **Real-time app** — a collector thread polls the ready bits (or blocks on a std wakeup) and drains asynchronously. Per-event cost is unchanged: store + increment, with the swap path (a few atomics) amortized once per buffer.

This also reconciles with the design.md sketch rather than fighting it: the planned `RingRecorder` was a ring of *deltas*; this is a ring of *buffers*, which is better — the collector gets contiguous batches, and it's literally zc-ring's buffer-shipping shape. And it generalizes past raw deltas: a "buffer" can be a histogram table, giving `HistogramRecorder` the same swap-based snapshot the design already wanted ("double-buffered counts or an epoch swap"). One handoff protocol, two payload types.

**The real design questions it opens:**

1. **Fixed capacity.** A no-alloc `no_std` registry means compile-time bounds: max probes, buffers per probe, buffer length — `Registry<const …>` owning all storage statically, probes claiming slots atomically at construction. Fine for embedded and benchmarks; the `std` layer can add a growable variant later if it ever matters.
2. **Backpressure.** If *free* is empty (collector behind), the probe must never block or overwrite — drop-and-count, same philosophy as `ArrayRecorder::dropped`. The count tells you the collector was undersized.
3. **The slow probe / partial buffer.** A low-rate probe may take arbitrarily long to fill a buffer, so READY-on-full alone starves the collector of its data. Simplest fix: a per-slot "flush requested" flag the registry sets and the probe checks with one relaxed load on its swap-or-record path, plus an explicit `flush()` for the benchmark's defined collection points. Collector-side *stealing* of a partial buffer breaks the single-writer rule — I'd avoid it.
4. **Identity and reclamation.** The registry slot index becomes the buffer's probe id (a legitimate rebirth of `site_id` — this time it's load-bearing), and probe drop needs a slot-release story so benchmarks can construct probes per run.

One thing to hold onto from the earlier design: the registry should be an app-constructed object you pass around (or a static the app declares), not hidden global state — that keeps "transport is the application's business" true, with the registry as within-process brokering only.

I think this is ready to write down. Want me to capture it as a design section (registry + buffer-cycling protocol, the four questions above) in `notes/design.md` or a chores subsection, and queue a Todo for the implementation ladder?

message.content: Yes, write it up in notes/design.md and add a Todo

file.content:
# tprobe — founding rationale & consolidation plan

This crate exists to end a four-way copy-paste. A tick-counter
measurement probe was vendored independently into four repos
(actor-x1, zc-ring-x1, iiac-perf, and prospectively zc-msg-x1)
and drifted in each. This note is the founding record: why we
are consolidating those copies into one crate, no_std-first, and
what that crate should be. It is the writeup of the design
discussion that created this repo.

## The problem: four divergent copies, none no_std

Three independent copies of the tick-probe exist, drifted apart —
not just vendored duplicates but three different scope decisions:

- **iiac-perf** (`src/tprobe.rs` + `src/tprobe2.rs` +
  `src/band_table.rs`, ~442 lines total, in the 0.20.0 binary) —
  a minimal probe core with the full calibrated harness built
  around it in the same crate and is the original version.
- **actor-x1** (`crates/tprobe`, v0.1.5) — the richest:
  `tprobe.rs` (~312 lines) folds `overhead.rs`
  (apparatus-overhead calibration) and `pin.rs` (CPU pinning)
  _into_ the probe crate. And is based on iiac-perf version.
- **zc-ring-x1** (`tprobe` v0.1.0 + sibling `tp_runner`) — split
  the other way: a bare `tprobe` crate (probe + `ticks` +
  `band_table` + `tprobe_span`) and a separate `tp_runner` crate
  holding the drive loop, pinning, and Linux `perf_event_open`
  counters. `tprobe.rs` is ~110 lines.
- **zc-msg-x1** — would have been a forth copy if its seeding
  plan (path-dep zc-ring-x1's `tprobe`/`tp_runner`) were followed
  as written. Consolidating here is what stops that.

Two facts fall out of comparing them:

- **The duplication is real and drifting.** Three live,
  independently-edited forks — one bundling pin+overhead, one
  splitting them out, one minimal — is the DRY trigger the
  earlier "extract when a second consumer materializes" note was
  waiting for. We think each fork happened because there was no
  shared crate to depend on at the time, so each repo vendored
  and then edited locally.
- **None of the copies is `no_std`.** Every one leans on
  `hdrhistogram` (std), `String` probe names, and std time /
  `minstant`. So today nothing here can measure on an embedded
  target — the copies are all host-only.

## Scope: a probe is not a harness

The existing code already draws a clean line, worth preserving:

- **Probe** (this crate) — read a hardware tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  Small, embeddable, ideally `no_std`.
- **Harness** (iiac-perf; zc-ring's `tp_runner`) — adaptive loop
  sizing, apparatus-overhead subtraction, thread pinning, a bench
  registry, `perf_event_open` counters. zc-ring's `tp_runner`
  doc says it plainly: "Deliberately not a benchmark harness …
  for that scale of machinery use iiac-perf."

The consolidation target is the **probe**. The harness layer
(sizing, calibration, registry) stays where it is — iiac-perf
owns host-side characterization and already produces the
published percentile tables.

## Why not an off-the-shelf benchmarking crate

The perf-book benchmarking page [[1]] lists criterion, divan,
hyperfine, iai/gungraun (callgrind), bencher, and rustc-perf.
None replaces the probe for the need that motivates it — a
nanosecond-scale, cross-core, tail-latency _distribution_ of a
lock-free hot path, measurable on the target:

- **criterion / divan** — host-std statistical microbench
  harnesses. Good for A/B + throughput at the _mean_; they do not
  surface deep tail bands (the n5..n10 / p.999_999_999 rows these
  measurements are about — batch-timing discards the
  distribution). Both are `std`, so neither runs on an embedded
  target. divan is additionally stale (last release April 2025).
- **iai / gungraun (callgrind)** — instruction-count / cache
  _simulation_. Noise-free and great for CI regression guards,
  but they model a single simulated run and cannot reproduce the
  real cross-core cache-line-transfer economics that is the whole
  point (the 26–40% SPSC-vs-MPSC gap zc-ring measured is a
  hardware effect) [[2]].
- **hyperfine** — whole-process wall-clock timing; wrong
  granularity for ns round-trips. **bencher** — a CI tracking
  layer, orthogonal to how the numbers are produced.

So for _host-side_ CI regression gating an off-the-shelf harness
would work, but iiac-perf already covers host-side, making a
second one largely redundant; and for _on-target_ embedded
measurement nothing off-the-shelf applies at all. This
home-grown probe is the only thing that can measure on the
target — which is why it is worth investing in rather than
replacing.

227	Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
228	consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
229	messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
230	replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
231	across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
232	requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
233	foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
234	zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
235	— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
236	later.
237	
238	## Consumers and migration
239	
240	This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
241	per-repo and not all at once:
242	
243	- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
244	  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
245	- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
246	  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
247	  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
248	  assuming it will depend on this crate.
249	- **zc-ring-x1** — frozen lab notebook; we think we will **not**
250	  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).
251	- **zc-msg-x1** — the new consumer; starts on this crate instead
252	  of a vendored copy.
253	
254	## Name
255	
256	Kept **`tprobe`** — already the crate name in all four repos
257	(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
258	what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
259	was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
260	continuity.
261	
262	## Open questions
263	
264	- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
265	  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
266	- Recorder trait shape: generic (static dispatch, monomorphized
267	  per recorder) vs. an enum? Affects hot-path codegen and the
268	  API every consumer sees.
269	- Snapshot wire format: encoding, versioning, and how the
270	  bucket-layout parameters travel with the counts.
271	- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
272	  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.
273	- Where do pinning and `perf_event_open` live — in the `std`
274	  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
275	  crate as zc-ring split them?
276	
277	# References
278	
279	[1]: https://nnethercote.github.io/perf-book/benchmarking.html
280	[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1

Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
later.

## Consumers and migration

This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
per-repo and not all at once:

- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
  assuming it will depend on this crate.

  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).

  of a vendored copy.

(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
continuity.

- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
- Recorder trait shape: generic (static dispatch, monomorphized
  per recorder) vs. an enum? Affects hot-path codegen and the
  API every consumer sees.
- Snapshot wire format: encoding, versioning, and how the
  bucket-layout parameters travel with the counts.
- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.

  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
  crate as zc-ring split them?

## Registry and buffer-cycling handoff

How collected data crosses from the hot path to analysis. The
Phases section left probe discovery implicit ("a collector
thread drains"); the decision is a **registry**: a probe
registers at construction and receives a set of buffers to
fill, and a collector harvests filled buffers through the
registry. One handoff protocol serves both use classes:

- **The protocol is buffer cycling.** A probe fills its
  current buffer with plain stores; when full it marks the
  buffer READY and takes a FREE one; the collector drains
  READY buffers and returns them FREE. Ownership is one atomic
  state per buffer slot (FILLING → READY → DRAINING → FREE).
  "Send the buffer" (queue view) and "set a bit the registry
  notices" (in-place view) are the same ownership transfer —
  in the `no_std` core both are just atomics; how the
  collector learns of READY (polling the bits, or an eventfd /
  condvar wakeup) is a `std`-layer add-on.
- **Benchmark drive** — collection points are well defined, so
  the "collector" is inline: the harness calls collect on the
  registry from its own thread after the loop. With one big
  buffer per probe this degenerates to exactly
  `ArrayRecorder` — the landed recorder is the 1-buffer
  special case.
- **Real-time drive** — collection is asynchronous: a
  collector thread polls the ready bits (or blocks on the std
  wakeup) and histograms off the measured path. Per-event cost
  is unchanged (store + increment); the swap path's few
  atomics amortize once per buffer.
- **Payloads generalize.** A buffer is raw deltas *or* a
  histogram table: the same handoff gives `HistogramRecorder`
  the double-buffered snapshot the Phases section calls for,
  and it reframes `RingRecorder` as a ring of *buffers* rather
  than of deltas. We think the buffer ring is the better
  shape — the collector reads contiguous batches, and it
  matches zc-ring's buffer-shipping domain.
- **The registry is app-visible, not ambient.** The app
  constructs it (or declares it static) and passes it to probe
  construction — within-process brokering only; transport
  stays out of scope, as before.

Design questions to settle in the implementing cycle:

- **Fixed capacity** — no-alloc `no_std` means compile-time
  bounds (max probes, buffers per probe, buffer length): a
  const-generic registry owning all storage, probes claiming
  slots atomically at construction. A growable `std` variant
  can layer on later if ever needed.
- **Backpressure** — FREE exhausted (collector behind) must
  never block or overwrite: drop-and-count, the
  `ArrayRecorder::dropped` philosophy.
- **Partial buffers** — a low-rate probe may never fill a
  buffer, starving the collector of its data. Plan: an
  explicit probe-side flush for defined collection points,
  plus a per-slot flush-request flag the probe checks with one
  relaxed load. Collector-side *stealing* of a partial buffer
  would break the single-writer rule — rejected.
- **Identity and reclamation** — the registry slot index is
  the buffer's probe id (a legitimate rebirth of the `site_id`
  the Spans bullet dissolved — this time load-bearing, since a
  harvested buffer outlives its fill site); probe drop needs a
  slot-release story so benchmarks can construct probes per
  run.

content.text:
This is you answering the sample-type question design.md deferred (the Spans bullet at `notes/design.md:133` punts "time-ordered tracing" to a "future recorder sample-type question"). The future arrived. My thoughts:

**The three types are points on the compression ladder, not three probes.** The organizing insight already in design.md — histogramming is compression, the recorder choice moves the compression point — extends cleanly one rung up. A timestamp series discards nothing; timestamp+duration discards idle detail between spans; a duration series discards position in time; a histogram discards everything but the distribution. Each type is the one below plus information, so "which type" is *what the recorder keeps*, not what the probe is. The probe's job barely changes — it reads ticks; the sample type lives in the recorder and, downstream, in the buffer payload tag of the wire format. That keeps this a recorder/buffer question, exactly where the design put it.

**Timestamp+duration is the general form, and cost is why the others exist.** `(start_ts, delta)` reconstructs both the duration series and (for the loop) the timestamp series; the reason not to always record it is bytes — 16 B/event vs 8, which matters at 10M events/s on an embedded link but is nothing at control-loop rates. So: distribution work keeps the cheap forms, flight recorder pays for the full form. At 100 Hz even a fat record is trivial bandwidth — ~50 bytes × 100/s = 5 KB/s.

**Your control-loop case is one probe, and the full form covers all three asks at once:**

- **Jitter** — consecutive start timestamps vs the 10 ms nominal grid. You don't need a separate timestamp probe for this; it falls out of the span records.
- **Processing time** — the duration, same record.
- **Phase breakdown** — the intermediate timestamps.

**Intermediate timestamps should be fixed-arity checkpoints.** A control loop has a known, fixed set of phases (wake → sense → compute → actuate), so a record of `start + K checkpoint deltas + end` with const-generic `K` gives fixed-size records — which the no-alloc buffer format needs anyway; variable-length records would poison the buffer-cycling design. API-wise that's `start()`, `checkpoint()` ×K, `record()` on the existing probe. And keeping an iteration as *one atomic record* is genuinely better than the alternative (a probe per phase): no cross-buffer alignment to reconstruct an iteration, no torn iterations at buffer boundaries.

**A flight-recorder bonus:** with timestamps, data loss becomes visible. Drop-and-count on a duration series tells you *how many* samples vanished; on a timestamp series the gap tells you *which iterations* and *when* — which is what you want when doing jitter forensics on the 100 Hz loop.

Two things to pin down when this gets written up:

1. **Point events** (the pure timestamp series, type two) need a probe verb — `mark()` — since there's no span. Cheap: one tick read, one store. Its compressed sibling (record inter-arrival delta instead of the timestamp) is just the duration recorder fed by `mark()`, another rung on the same ladder.
2. **Wire format impact** — the snapshot/buffer format now needs a sample-type descriptor (duration / timestamp / span / span+K), and `K` is part of the layout parameters, same as bucket layout is for histograms.

Nothing here disturbs the registry + buffer-cycling section — buffers just gain a typed payload, and the flight-recorder path is "full-form records, pushed asynchronously" which is precisely the real-time drive. Want me to fold this into design.md as a "Sample types" section (and touch the registry section's payload bullet to point at it)?

message.content: And one other twist, for systems without storage or high bandwidth egress capability, i.e. deeply embedded, I want to "fast" histogram information like the original tproble where record was directly to the histogram. And critically we'll be able to measure the performance costs of the various solutions, record to RAM, record to storage, record directly to histogram.

content.text:
Good news: this twist lands on ground the design already holds — but it sharpens two things worth recording. Mapping it:

**The "fast" histogram-direct mode is already committed.** `HistogramRecorder` in the Phases section is exactly the original-tprobe behavior you're describing: in-place log-linear bucket increment on record, for "embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots instead." In ladder terms it's the bottom rung — compression pushed all the way into `record()` itself. And it composes with the registry design as-is: the "buffer" is a histogram table, the probe cycles two of them, and the READY snapshot is the fixed-size egress unit — a deeply embedded target ships a few-hundred-byte snapshot at whatever cadence its link tolerates, or never ships and is read out post-mortem.

**Sample types × compression compose, which rescues embedded jitter measurement.** Histogram-direct discards time ordering, so no flight recorder — but you can histogram *any* sample stream, including inter-arrival deltas from `mark()`. That gives the 100 Hz loop's jitter *distribution* on-device at histogram cost, no raw timestamps needed. Each probe picks its own point in the (sample type × compression) grid per deployment: host benchmark takes raw spans, embedded takes histogrammed durations and histogrammed inter-arrivals.

**The cost comparison is already seeded, and your framing upgrades it.** The "First experiment: recorder cost" section says comparing recorder impls is the first experiment tprobe runs against itself — including the recorded hypothesis that the raw store wins per-event but the histogram's L1-resident table could invert the ranking on long runs via cache pressure. Your three-way framing turns that into a proper cost matrix, and I'd split it along a line the experiment needs anyway:

- **Hot-path cost per event** — what the probed code pays: store-to-RAM (array/buffer) vs histogram increment. Measurable by tprobe itself: same measured op, swap the recorder (the `Recorder` trait makes this a one-line A/B), and the `outer − sum(inner)` cross-check self-measures the apparatus.
- **System cost** — what the machine pays around it: collector CPU, memory bandwidth, and storage/egress writes. "Record to storage" lives here, not on the hot path — no recorder should touch storage in `record()`; storage is where the *collector* drains READY buffers to, and its cost is a collector-side measurement (with its own probe, naturally).

One consequence for the trait-shape open question: a fair A/B needs the recorder dispatch itself to cost nothing, which argues for the generic/monomorphized side of that question, at least for the measurement builds.

So nothing to redesign — this turn confirms `HistogramRecorder`'s reason to exist and promotes the recorder-cost experiment from "first experiment" to a deliverable with a defined matrix. ## Sample types and the compression ladder

What the recorder keeps per event is its own axis, independent
of where compression happens. Three sample types cover the
uses that have surfaced:

- **Duration** — the landed `start()`…`record()` delta,
  8 B/event. Distribution measurement; discards position in
  time.
- **Timestamp** — a raw tick per point event, via a new probe
  verb `mark()` (one tick read, one store). Inter-arrival and
  ordering measurement; discards nothing. Feeding
  `mark()`-to-`mark()` deltas to a duration recorder instead
  records inter-arrivals — same rung as duration.
- **Timestamp + duration** — `(start_ts, delta)` span
  records, 16 B/event. The flight-recorder form, and the
  general one: it reconstructs both the start-timestamp series
  and the duration series; its byte cost is the only reason
  the cheaper types exist.

These rungs form a compression ladder, and the histogram of
the Phases section is its bottom rung — compression inside
`record()` itself, discarding everything but the distribution.
The two axes compose: any sample stream can be histogrammed,
so a deeply embedded target (no storage, no high-bandwidth
egress) keeps a jitter distribution (histogrammed `mark()`
inter-arrivals) and a processing-time distribution on-device
at fixed size — the original tprobe's
record-directly-to-histogram mode, preserved as
`HistogramRecorder`. Each probe picks its own (sample type ×
compression) cell per deployment.

The motivating flight-recorder case is a 100 Hz control loop,
where one span probe covers three asks with one record:

- **Jitter** — consecutive start timestamps against the 10 ms
  nominal grid; no separate timestamp probe needed.
- **Processing time** — the span duration, same record.
- **Phase breakdown** — intermediate checkpoints inside the
  span: `start()`, `checkpoint()` ×K, `record()` yields a
  record of `start + K checkpoint deltas + end`. `K` is
  const-generic so records stay fixed-size — variable-length
  records would poison the no-alloc buffer format. One atomic
  record per iteration also beats a probe per phase: no
  cross-buffer alignment to reconstruct an iteration, no torn
  iterations at buffer boundaries.
- **Loss visibility** — drop-and-count on a duration series
  says how many samples vanished; timestamps show *which*
  iterations and when — what jitter forensics wants.

At 100 Hz even the fat checkpointed record is ~5 KB/s,
trivial to push asynchronously to storage; the same full form
at 10M events/s is ~160 MB/s — why the ladder is chosen
per-probe, not per-crate. Wire-format consequence: the
snapshot/buffer payload needs a sample-type descriptor
(duration / timestamp / span / span+K), with `K` a layout
parameter just as the bucket layout is for histograms.

## Registry and buffer-cycling handoff

1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
4	contains instructions on how a commit cycle is accomplished.
5	
6	The artifact a cycle produces is whatever the bot generates from
7	the conversation — code, prose, an image, a song, a screenplay.
8	The steps below use a Rust crate as the running example (the
9	cargo cycle, `Cargo.toml` versioning); substitute your medium's
10	equivalents — this project's manifest is recorded in
11	[versioning.md](versioning.md).
12	
13	## Cycles
14	
15	A cycle has three phases:
16	
17	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
18	  first commit, when it needs setup (a lightweight cycle omits
19	  it and starts at `-1` — see
20	  [versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)). Sets up the
21	  cycle:
22	  - Bump the version-of-record to `X.Y.Z-0` (where it lives
23	    and the suffix scheme are project-specific — see
24	    [versioning.md](versioning.md)).
25	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
26	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
27	    statement + plan ladder).
28	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
29	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
30	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
31	  needs; each runs through the
32	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
33	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
34	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
35	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
36	    `## Done`.
37	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
38	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
39	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
40	    changed.
41	
42	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
43	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
44	result must always be published at close-out. See
45	[Pushing](#pushing).
46	
47	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
48	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
49	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
50	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
51	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
52	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
53	for the local-ladder mechanics.
54	
55	## Chores sections
56	
57	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
58	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording landed work. In
59	general, every commit that lands on the permanent branch
60	should have a reference to it on a `Commits:` list in a
61	chores file.
62	
63	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
64	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
65	with the title it records (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar`),
66	followed by an **empty `Commits:`** line. The narrative
67	body stays empty until close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out));
68	the `Commits:` line is backfilled later, once the commit is
69	permanent (see [Commits backfill](#commits-backfill) below).
70	
71	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
72	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
73	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
74	
75	### Commits backfill
76	
77	A chores section's `Commits:` line cites the commit(s) it
78	records, by SHA — but a SHA isn't stable until the commit
79	lands on a **permanent branch** (`main`, or a long-lived
80	release/patch branch that won't be rewritten); a rebase or
81	squash rewrites it on the way. So:
82	
83	- A chores section is **opened with an empty `Commits:`** line.
84	- **Backfill once the commit is on a permanent branch**,
85	  where its SHA is final. A commit can't record its own SHA
86	  (that would change the hash), so the fill always lands one
87	  push later: **each push backfills the `Commits:` of the
88	  commits the previous push made permanent.** On a topic
89	  branch the sections instead wait until the branch lands —
90	  so no SHA is ever written that a later rebase could
91	  invalidate.
92	
93	Use `[[N]]` refs — several as `[[N]],[[M]]` only when one
94	section records multiple commits (a merge non-ff close-out) —
95	with the commit URL + 40-hex SHA in the file's `# References`
96	(format in AGENTS.md
97	[Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references)).
98	A section's `##` title matches its commit title, so a rare
99	deliberate rewrite of a permanent-branch commit re-syncs via
100	`git log --grep "<title>"`.
101	
102	The per-push cadence is a project choice, not dogma — a
103	**per-close-out** model (recording a cycle's SHAs at its
104	close-out) is equally valid. The one invariant: a recorded
105	SHA must be permanent.
106	
107	## Preparation
108	
109	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`), when the cycle needs
110	setup (a lightweight cycle omits it — see
111	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering)):
112	
113	- **Bump the version-of-record** to `X.Y.Z-0`. Where it
114	  lives, the suffix scheme, and any derived files (a
115	  lockfile, a sourced manifest version) are
116	  project-specific — see [versioning.md](versioning.md).
117	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
118	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
119	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
120	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
121	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
122	  - A **plan ladder**.
123	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
124	  append a `##` header with the title it records — the
125	  cycle's anticipated close-out title.
126	
127	## Work-N
128	
129	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
130	implement the change. As many as needed:
131	
132	- Each commit runs through the
133	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
134	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
135	  visibility).
136	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
137	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
138	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
139	  scope grows enough (see
140	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
141	
142	## Close-out
143	
144	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
145	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
146	what happens post-squash:
147	
148	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
149	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
150	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
151	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
152	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
153	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
154	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
155	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
156	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
157	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
158	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
159	    back-reference.
160	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
161	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
162	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
163	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
164	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
165	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
166	
167	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
168	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
169	see [Pushing](#pushing).
170	
171	## Numbering
172	
173	Each commit's phase is encoded in the version suffix — `-0`
174	Preparation, `-1`/`-2`/… Work, bare `X.Y.Z` Close-out,
175	recursively for sub-cycles. The full scheme — disambiguation,
176	nesting, optional Preparation, the project's version-of-record
177	format, and the per-phase bump — lives in
178	[versioning.md](versioning.md#step-numbering), which is the
179	single source of truth for this repo's versioning.
180	
181	## Per-commit flow
182	
183	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
184	through:
185	
186	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
187	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
188	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
189	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
190	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
191	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
192	4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
193	   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
194	   example the cargo cycle is:
195	   1. `cargo fmt`
196	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
197	   3. `cargo test`
198	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
199	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
200	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
201	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
202	   changes and we iterate until complete.
203	6. **Write the commit description** — see
204	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
205	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
206	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
207	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
208	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
209	   keeps the verb visible):
210	
211	   ```
212	   jj commit -m \
213	   "<type>: <short description>" \
214	   -m "<intro paragraph>
215	
216	   - file1: gist
217	   - file2: gist
218	
219	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
220	   -R .
221	   ```
222	
223	**Two overrides apply:**
224	
225	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
226	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
227	  surface it; don't push through.
228	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
229	  a review or question forward.
230	
231	## Commit description
232	
233	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):
234	
235	```
236	<type>: <short description>
237	```
238	
239	Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
240	version-of-record — where it lives and its bump cadence, see
241	[versioning.md](versioning.md) — is useful for confirming
242	you're running the version you're testing, not a per-commit
243	title marker. We think per-commit version-in-title is
244	unstable on large projects: many changes are in flight
245	simultaneously, and a
246	version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
247	titles don't carry one.
248	
249	### Title
250	
251	- ≤50 chars total.
252	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
253	  `docs`, `chore`.
254	- Favor terse phrasings.
255	- **Distinct per step** — each of a cycle's commits gets its
256	  own descriptive title (no shared cycle title with a step
257	  marker). Share a greppable stem across the cycle's titles
258	  (e.g. `ring buffer`) so `git log --grep` collects them; the
259	  chores section header matches the close-out title. See
260	  AGENTS.md
261	  [Conventional-commit shape](../AGENTS.md#conventional-commit-shape-ladder--chores--commit).
262	
263	### Body
264	
265	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
266	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
267	
268	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
269	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
270	  files with multiple distinct changes:
271	
272	  ```
273	  - path/to/file1
274	    - first distinct change
275	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
276	       with continuation indented 5
277	  ```
278	
279	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
280	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
281	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
282	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
283	
284	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
285	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
286	
287	### Trailer
288	
289	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
290	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
291	in AGENTS.md for the convention.
292	
293	For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
294	trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
295	space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
296	hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.
297	
298	## Reviewing changes
299	
300	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
301	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
302	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
303	
304	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
305	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
306	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
307	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
308	
309	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
310	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
311	`jj show -r @-`.
312	
313	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
314	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
315	are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).
316	
317	## Pushing
318	
319	### Policy
320	
321	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
322	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
323	the cycle's result must be published.
324	
325	**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
326	cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
327	happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
328	published and explicitly approved that specific push.
329	Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
330	the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
331	
332	**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
333	the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
334	commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
335	hard stop after push/finalize — are the *interactive default*.
336	They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
337	bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
338	and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
339	through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
340	continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:
341	
342	- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
343	  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
344	  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
345	  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
346	  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
347	  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
348	  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
349	  unattended") waives them.
350	- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
351	  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
352	- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
353	  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
354	  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
355	  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
356	  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
357	  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
358	  reaches this.
359	- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
360	  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
361	- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
362	  per-push approval.
363	
364	### Shape at close-out push
365	
366	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
367	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
368	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
369	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
370	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
371	
372	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
373	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
374	  focused on one or two files.
375	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
376	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
377	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
378	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
379	  for the full setup sequence.
380	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
381	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
382	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
383	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
384	
385	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
386	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
387	
388	### Merge non-ff recipe
389	
390	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
391	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
392	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
393	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
394	Work commit:
395	
396	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
397	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
398	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
399	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
400	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
401	
402	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
403	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
404	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
405	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
406	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
407	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
408	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
409	
410	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
411	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
412	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
413	push force-updates `main`.
414	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
415	the close-out push.
416	
417	### vc-x1 push wrapper
418	
419	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
420	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
421	
422	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
423	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
424	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
425	planned:
426	
427	- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
428	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
429	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
430	
431	### .claude cadence
432	
433	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
434	with every code commit in that push.
435	
436	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
437	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
438	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
439	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
440	
441	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
442	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
443	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
444	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
445	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
446	
447	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
448	session information created, which we don't worry about.
449	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
450	remain empty.
451	
452	### Bot communication at the reviews
453	
454	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
455	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
456	
457	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
458	  "Work complete. Please review."
459	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
460	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
461	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
462	  --body ...` invocation by default.
463	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
464	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
465	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
466	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
467	
468	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
469	
470	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
471	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
472	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
473	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
474	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
475	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
476	step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
477	stop for the whole turn.
478	
479	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
480	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
481	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
482	closing words *before* invoking it. The harness rejects an empty
483	turn, so it may force a visible token after the tool returns; if
484	so, emit a bare acknowledgment only (e.g. "landed") — never a
485	summary, verification, or next-step offer.
486	
487	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
488	squashes → pushes):
489	
490	- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
491	  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
492	  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
493	  the invoke — into the snapshot.
494	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
495	  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
496	  the bot to do more work.
497	- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
498	  after the tool returns. The harness may force a visible token
499	  (it rejects an empty turn) — keep it to a bare acknowledgment,
500	  never a summary or a next step.
501	
502	**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
503	finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
504	saying, say it *before* the invoke.
505	
506	### Recovery
507	
508	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
509	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
510	  finalize by hand:
511	
512	  ```
513	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
514	  ```
515	
516	- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
517	  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
518	  extra activity by the bot's agents).
519	  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
520	    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
521	    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
522	    *after* the snapshot.
523	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
524	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
525	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
526	  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
527	    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
528	    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
529	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
530	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
531	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
532	    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
533	    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
534	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
535	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
536	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
537	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
538	  stale stage.
539	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
540	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
541	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
542	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
543	  push.
544	
545	## Iterative work
546	
547	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
548	from incremental review, loop:
549	
550	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
551	2. Make the next round of changes.
552	3. User reviews the round (see
553	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
554	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
555	   new empty `@`.
556	5. If not done, go to step 2.
557	
558	Same jj mechanics as a
559	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
560	single-commit scope — the version
561	doesn't change.
562	
563	## Sub-cycle ladders
564	
565	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
566	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
567	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
568	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
569	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
570	for review and bisection only.
571	
572	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
573	
574	For each Work commit in the ladder:
575	
576	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
577	2. Do the commit's work.
578	3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
579	   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
580	   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
581	   suite, raising bisection cost.
582	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
583	   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
584	   into one final commit.
585	
586	### Navigating the ladder
587	
588	Common moves:
589	
590	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
591	  from its base.
592	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
593	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
594	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
595	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
596	  isolation.
597	
598	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
599	descendants auto-rebase.
600	
601	### Close-out: squash the ladder
602	
603	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
604	
605	```
606	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
607	```
608	
609	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
610	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
611	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
612	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
613	
614	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
615	
616	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
617	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
618	
619	### Recovery
620	
621	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
622	prior commits:
623	
624	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
625	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
626	  parent.
627	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
628	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
629	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
630	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
631	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
632	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
633	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
634	
635	# References
636	
637	- [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets) — revset primitives
638	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
639	- The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because a
640	  regression introduced in an early ladder commit can go
641	  uncaught until a later commit runs the full suite, raising
642	  bisection cost.
643
